title: Impeaching Amir Hatem Mahdy Ali, a judge of the United States District Court for the District of Columbia, for high crimes and misdemeanors.
plain_summary: The House of Representatives wants to remove Judge Amir Hatem Mahdy Ali from his job as a federal judge. They say he did something wrong by making a court order that stopped President Trump from pausing government money that was supposed to go to other countries. The House believes the judge should not have interfered with the president's power to control this spending. They also think the judge did not think about safety concerns when he ordered the money to be paid right away.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution calls for the impeachment of Amir Hatem Mahdy Ali. He is a judge for the United States District Court for the District of Columbia. The House of Representatives is charging him with high crimes and misdemeanors (serious wrongdoing by government officials).

sections.title: Resolution Details
sections.text: Representative Ogles from Tennessee and Representative Gill from Texas introduced this resolution on February twenty seventh, twenty twenty five. The resolution was sent to the House Judiciary Committee for review.

sections.title: Impeachment Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives has decided that Judge Amir Hatem Mahdy Ali should be impeached for high crimes and misdemeanors. They are sending one article of impeachment to the Senate. The House is acting on behalf of itself and the American people in bringing these charges against Judge Ali.

sections.title: Article of Impeachment
sections.text: Judge Ali has acted in ways that go against the trust and confidence that should be placed in a federal judge. In twenty twenty four, Judge Ali told the Senate Judiciary Committee that judges must decide cases based on fair and objective application of the law. However, his later actions contradicted this statement. Judge Ali issued a temporary restraining order that stopped the President from pausing certain government funding. This order was created through Executive Order fourteen one six nine. The House claims that Judge Ali wrongly interfered with the President's constitutional power to conduct foreign policy and review federal agencies and programs. The House says this violation of constitutional rules cannot be explained by ignorance of the law and makes him unfit to serve as a district court judge.

sections.title: Claims About Funding Disbursement
sections.text: The House also charges that Judge Ali ordered immediate payment of funds in a way that was arbitrary and capricious (unreasonable and without proper consideration). The President's executive order was meant to review these funds to make sure they matched United States foreign policy goals. By ordering immediate payment of funds that the President had paused, Judge Ali did not consider the troubled history of foreign aid through the United States Agency for International Development (USAID). A March twenty twenty one Government Accountability Office report showed that from fiscal year twenty fifteen to twenty nineteen, USAID did not consistently make sure that money given for projects in the Gaza Strip and Judea and Samaria followed rules designed to prevent financial support for terrorism. More recently, in November twenty twenty four, USAID was found to have paid for hundreds of thousands of meals for fighters connected to al-Qaida in Syria. While Judge Ali argued that the Trump administration funding pause caused irreparable harm (damage that cannot be fixed), he failed to consider that his decision could easily cause irreparable harm to Americans and American interests.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.text: The House concludes that Judge Amir Hatem Mahdy Ali has acted in a way that completely lacks intellectual honesty and basic integrity. They say he is guilty of high crimes and misdemeanors, is unfit to hold the office of federal judge, and should be removed from office.
